title: The Fire That Does Not Consume
subtitle: Pentecost: the Holy Spirit descends in tongues of flame upon the disciples

stillness: Sit in the upper room with the rest, persevering in prayer,
lifted up toward the things above by desire.
gospel: On the day of Pentecost, the fiftieth from the Resurrection,
they were all together in one place.
And suddenly there came a sound from heaven,
as of a violent wind rushing in,
and it filled the whole house where they were sitting.
And there appeared to them parted tongues, as it were of fire,
and it sat upon each one of them.
And they were all filled with the Holy Spirit,
and they began to speak in diverse tongues,
as the Spirit gave them to utter.
behold: Attend now to each thing, as if you were present.
Behold the disciples, a hundred and twenty, gathered as one,
the twelve of the Apostles now made tenfold,
all in one place, persevering in prayer,
awaiting the sending of the Holy Spirit.
Hear, at the third hour of the day, the sudden sound out of heaven,
a crash that resounds as of a rushing, vehement wind.
See the fire divided into tongues:
rays of flame, in the likeness of tongues, resting upon their heads,
and one settles upon each single one of them.
consider: Well, as Gregory says, did the Spirit appear in fire,
for from every heart that He fills He shakes out the torpor of cold,
and kindles it in the desire of His own eternity.
Our God is a consuming fire, says Origen,
as long as there are in us things worthy to be consumed;
but when they have been consumed, He will be a fire no longer consuming, but enlightening.
Fire has a twofold nature, says Jerome: it gives light, and it burns;
so too God gives light to the just, and burns the sinner.
He is rightly called the vehement Spirit,
as one carrying us out of our mind, drawing us away from the mind's affections,
and as one taking woe away, bearing off eternal damnation.
He came in the sound, to terrify the rebellious,
and in the breath, which proceeds from within, to give life to the affections of the devout.
He first purges, by the gift of fear;
He melts, by the gift of piety;
He adorns, by the gift of knowledge;
He makes firm, by the gift of fortitude;
He lifts up, by the gift of counsel;
He enlightens, by the gift of understanding;
He sweetens, by the gift of wisdom.
To the mouth He gave the word, to the understanding light,
to the affection ardor, and to the heart strength.
The Church was then in one nation;
but when they spoke in the tongues of all, it was shown
that the Church, growing through all the nations, would speak in the tongues of all,
for now the Church holds the tongues of all.
prayer: O Holy Spirit, the Spirit of truth,
come now and descend, fill us and console us,
and bestow upon us the fullness of virtues and of joys.
make firm our weakness by fortitude,
and with the inextinguishable ardor of charity kindle us to all love.
Consume in us whatever is worthy to be consumed,
and when it is consumed, be in us no longer a fire that consumes, but one that enlightens.
Give light to the just, and to this wretched sinner give it also;
carry us out of ourselves, and draw us away from the affections of the mind,
and bear off from us the eternal damnation we have deserved.

latinJewel.latin: Bene in igne Spiritus apparuit, quia ab omni corde quod replet, torporem frigoris excutit, et hoc in desiderio suæ æternitatis accendit.
latinJewel.english: Well did the Spirit appear in fire, for from every heart that He fills He shakes out the torpor of cold, and kindles it in the desire of His own eternity.
